nativeRange.habitat: The Coral Hawkfish is a reef-associated species that tends to rest on, in, or beneath stony and soft corals in clear lagoon, channel, and seaward reef environments, at depths of roughly 1–40 m (typically 10–25 m).

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, mysis, krill, artemia) and small fishes. Generally accepts a varied diet of frozen meaty marine preparations and, less ideally, live feeder shrimp in captivity; small portions delivered two to three times daily are commonly advised.

summary: The Coral Hawkfish is a small Indo-Pacific reef percher known for its whitish body and bold red spots. It is generally considered undemanding in terms of water quality, but tends to be aggressive toward small tankmates and is an accomplished jumper that requires a tightly covered tank.
careNotes:
The Coral Hawkfish (Cirrhitichthys oxycephalus) is sold under several trade names — 'Pixy Hawkfish', 'Spotted Hawkfish', and 'Pixie Hawkfish' — all referring to the same species. It is typically described as having a whitish to pale-blue body marked with large red or red-brown spots. Specimens tend to perch motionlessly on coral branches, rockwork, or the substrate, ambushing prey rather than actively swimming — a behavior linked to a reduced or absent swimbladder that makes them 'hop' between resting spots rather than swim conventionally.

The Coral Hawkfish is generally considered reef-safe with caution: corals are typically left undisturbed, but ornamental shrimp (such as cleaner shrimp and peppermint shrimp), small hermit crabs, and small fish are at significant risk of predation. It is commonly advised to keep only one hawkfish per tank and to introduce it last, as it has been known to harass new tankmates aggressively, including fish considerably larger than itself. Bonded pairs are occasionally reported when two individuals are introduced simultaneously, but this is not the norm.

The species is an accomplished jumper that has been observed darting upward with sudden bursts of speed; a tightly fitting lid or mesh cover is generally considered essential. In the wild, the Coral Hawkfish is documented as territorial and haremic, with males presiding over a small group of females, and as a pelagic spawner (meaning gametes are released into open water) with brief spawning ascents into the water column.

Adult size in aquaria commonly falls in the 8–12 cm range, with a documented wild maximum of 15.2 cm. Although some sources rate this species as easy to keep, the main practical challenges are managing its aggression, maintaining a securely covered tank, and selecting compatible tankmates carefully — not any particular sensitivity to water quality. Hawkfishes in this family are generally documented as protogynous hermaphrodites, meaning individuals begin life as females and may transition to male.
breedingNotes: Not commonly bred in captivity. Wild spawning has been documented at dusk in pelagic fashion, with mated pairs ascending 0.3–1.0 m into the water column to release gametes.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page. Provided size (3 in / 7.6 cm), tank size (30 gal / 114 L), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), temperament (Aggressive), reef compatibility (With Caution). Reached via the LiveAquaria hawkfish collection root.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cirrhitidae, Centrarchiformes), maximum length 15.2 cm SL, depth 1–40 m (typical 10–25 m), wild temperature 24–28°C, Indo-Pacific + Eastern Pacific distribution, carnivorous diet (crustaceans and small fishes), territorial/haremic behavior, and pelagic spawning.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed adult size ~10 cm, minimum tank ~100 L, temperature ~26°C, SG ~1.026, carnivore, reef-safe with caution, hardy/suitable with care. Noted possibility of bonded-pair coexistence if introduced simultaneously. Reef2Reef wiki (designated first secondary in source-urls.json) returned no species-specific page; FishBase used as the primary secondary instead.

additional.name: Fishkeeper
additional.notes: Third cross-check. Confirmed adult size 10 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, territorial 'confident and pugnacious' temperament, reef safety with caution. Independently documented the jumping behavior and recommendation for a covered tank, and noted incompatibility with dottybacks, firefish, and gobies.
